name: Luning
short_description: BLM public rockhounding site in Nevada's high desert offering agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. Free collecting on gentle slopes near historic mining country.
description:
Luning is a remote BLM rockhounding destination in Mineral County, Nevada, situated at 4710 feet elevation in the high desert environment of west-central Nevada. This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene volcanic activity and subsequent hydrothermal processes deposited silica-rich minerals throughout the region, creating excellent conditions for agate and chalcedony formation. The site features gentle slopes and accessible terrain that makes it suitable for rockhounders of varying experience levels seeking free, unrestricted collecting opportunities.

The mineralization at Luning reflects the region's rich geothermal history. Agate nodules and chalcedony specimens occur in the surrounding washes and hillsides, where weathering and erosion have exposed colorful specimens ranging from translucent whites and creams to rust-orange and reddish varieties. Jasper and common quartz are also found throughout the area. The gentle slope terrain means you can cover substantial ground without extreme physical demands, making this a practical location for families and casual collectors.

Visitors should expect open BLM land with sparse vegetation typical of Nevada's high desert. The area is productive year-round, though fall through spring offers the most comfortable collecting conditions. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and basic tools like a rock hammer and collection bag. The remote location means services are limited, so plan accordingly.
history: This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene volcanic activity deposited silica-rich fluids that formed the agate and chalcedony specimens found here today. Mineral County's history of silver and gold mining throughout the late 1800s and early 1900s brought prospectors into this region, and their work exposed mineralized zones that rockhounders continue to access on public BLM land.
tips: Search in washes and arroyos where water erosion has concentrated specimens and exposed new material - focus on areas showing orange, rust, and cream-colored stones. Use a 3-4 pound rock hammer and chisel to extract specimens from host rock; many agates are found partially embedded in chalcedony or chert matrices. Look for translucent areas at the edges of nodules and for distinctive waxy luster that indicates quality specimens. Early morning or late afternoon collecting allows you to spot specimens more easily as side-lighting reveals color and texture.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4710 feet in high desert with gentle slopes and sparse low shrub vegetation. Terrain is relatively open with scattered outcrops and washes. Walking distance from parking areas typically ranges from short walks to 1-2 mile hikes depending on how far you venture. Footing is generally stable but rocky; sturdy hiking boots are recommended. The landscape is relatively exposed with minimal shade, so heat protection is important even in cooler months.
best_season: October - April. Nevada's high desert locations are most comfortable during fall and spring months when daytime temperatures are moderate (50-70 degrees F) and the intense summer heat (often exceeding 90 degrees F) is absent. Even mild winter days in this region are productive for rockhounding.
written_directions: From Hawthorne, Nevada (the nearest town with services), take US 95 north approximately 30 miles toward Mina. Continue past Mina for roughly 15 miles on US 95. Luning is accessed via local roads branching west from US 95 in the Mineral County area. The exact access point depends on specific claims and current BLM conditions; consult local BLM office or recent trip reports for precise pullout locations and coordinates. High-clearance vehicles are recommended for rough local roads.
road_conditions: US 95 is paved throughout. Local roads to specific collecting areas may be unpaved, potentially rough, and require moderate clearance. Conditions vary seasonally.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along access roads near productive collecting areas. No developed parking facilities. Park on BLM land away from private claims and active roads.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation equipment. Respect all posted claim markers and private property boundaries. All specimens must be hand-collected only.
rules:

Hand collection only
nearest_services: Hawthorne, Nevada (approximately 45 miles south) has gas, food, water, and supplies. Mina, Nevada (approximately 20 miles south) has limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved roads may have rough sections requiring high-clearance vehicles. Informal parking areas with no facilities. Sturdy footwear recommended for rocky terrain. Not wheelchair accessible due to road conditions and uneven ground.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Luning, Nevada?
faq.answer: Luning is known for agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz. Specimens range from translucent white and cream colors to rust-orange and reddish varieties. Many agates occur as nodules partially embedded in chalcedony matrix, making the site excellent for collectors of all levels seeking quality specimens without fees.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Luning?
faq.answer: No permit is required at Luning. The site is located on BLM public land where recreational mineral collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You can collect free of charge, but you must follow the 25 lb per day limit and personal use only restrictions.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Luning?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Nevada's high desert location means summer temperatures exceed 90 degrees F, making collecting uncomfortable. Fall and spring offer moderate daytime temperatures (50-70 degrees F) and pleasant conditions for spending hours searching hillsides and washes.

faq.question: How far is Luning from the nearest town with services?
faq.answer: Hawthorne, Nevada is the nearest town with full services including gas, food, and water, located approximately 45 miles south via US 95. Mina, Nevada is closer at about 20 miles south but offers only limited services. Plan accordingly and bring extra water for the remote site.

faq.question: What tools do you need to collect agate at Luning?
faq.answer: Bring a 3-4 pound rock hammer, cold chisel, collection bag or bucket, and sturdy gloves. Many specimens at Luning are partially embedded in host rock and require careful extraction. Sun protection, plenty of water, and hiking boots for rocky terrain are also essential for this remote location.

faq.question: Is Luning, Nevada a good rockhounding site for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Luning is beginner-friendly. The gentle slopes, easy terrain, and abundant surface specimens make it accessible for rockhounders new to the hobby. No permits or fees are required, and the free BLM access allows you to collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use. Family groups and children ages 6 and up can enjoy productive collecting here.
